claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you work through this. To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** can generally qualify automatically
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for your help! I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! That's very helpful information. 

So you're asking about your son **Alex, who is 8 years old**. Since Alex is under 16, that's a good start.

Let me move to the next check in the process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I imagine he's not in paid work at all, which is absolutely fine - this question is mainly relevant for older teenagers. Can you confirm Alex is not working 24+ hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - he's not working at all. He's just a kid, still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

What I mean is - is Alex receiving any benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit **in his own name**? 

This is quite rare for an 8-year-old, but I need to check. For most young children, the answer is that they are NOT receiving such benefits themselves.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just 8, so that wouldn't apply to him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Okay, next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Again, since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is no - apprenticeships are typically for teenagers aged 16+. But I need to check this as part of the assessment process.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - he's only 8 and still in primary school. Apprenticeships are for much older kids!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important part of Child Benefit eligibility.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme AND you're:
   - Working and earning above the National Insurance threshold, OR
   - Actively seeking work (for up to 91 days), OR
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself financially (and not claiming certain benefits), OR
   - Studying with sufficient resources, OR
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these situations apply to you?** Most people simply normally live in the UK - is that your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - we live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Living in Manchester means you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify as being responsible if **ANY** of these apply:

1. **You live with Alex** (this is the most common situation)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards his care (this can include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount

**Do any of these apply to you?** I'm guessing you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check something important:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time. For example:
- If Alex's other parent is already claiming for him
- If another family member is claiming for him

The possible situations are:
- **No one else is claiming** for Alex
- Someone else is claiming, but **you've agreed they'll transfer** the claim to you
- There's a **dispute** about who should claim (if so, HMRC would need to decide)

What's your situation - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Where is Alex currently living?**

I need to know if any of these special situations apply:
- Is Alex **in hospital**?
- Is Alex **in local authority care**?
- Is Alex **fostered**?
- Or is Alex **living normally at home** with you?

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, I'm assuming he's living normally at home - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he's living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words - is the council paying for Alex's living costs or upkeep? This would be things like the council paying for his accommodation or maintenance costs.

For most families where the child lives at home with the parent, the answer is that the council is NOT paying for the child. Is that your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything for Alex. He just lives at home with me and I support him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me move to the final stage.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✅

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for your son Alex.

## Why You're Eligible:

- ✅ **Alex qualifies** as he's 8 years old (under 16)
- ✅ **You meet residency requirements** - you normally live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - he lives at home with you
- ✅ **No conflict with other claimants** - no one else is claiming for Alex
- ✅ **Council not contributing** - the local council isn't paying for Alex's upkeep

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay a tax charge (though you can still claim the benefit)
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

## More Information:

You can find full details on how to claim here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in Manchester (UK residency met), the claimant is responsible for him as they live together, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, he's living normally at home, and the local council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. They normally live in the UK (Manchester), are responsible for Alex who lives with them at home, no other person is claiming for Alex, and the local council is not contributing to his upkeep. Alex meets the qualifying child criteria as he is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in Manchester (UK residency met), the claimant is responsible for him as they live together, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, he's living normally at home, and the local council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. They normally live in the UK (Manchester), are responsible for Alex who lives with them at home, no other person is claiming for Alex, and the local council is not contributing to his upkeep. Alex meets the qualifying child criteria as he is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship.
